era_context: The late 1880s sat at the apex of the 'New Imperialism,' as European powers raced to partition Africa and consolidate colonial footholds in Southeast Asia. In Europe, Bismarck's alliance system was at its most intricate, balancing the Triple Alliance with secret reassurance to Russia while managing a persistent war scare with France. Industrialisation was transforming economies, urban landscapes, and labour relations across the Atlantic world, while Meiji Japan pursued rapid modernisation and treaty revision. In China, the Qing dynasty faced mounting internal and environmental crises even as it attempted limited self-strengthening reforms.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Sub-Saharan Africa beyond European colonial contact zones
Central Asian communities under Russian imperial rule
Indigenous perspectives in settler-colonial states (United States, Australia, Canada)
Pacific Islands beyond major colonial ports
Rural Latin America outside capital cities and export economies
Korean internal politics beyond Chinese and Japanese diplomatic records

events.title: Interstate Commerce Act signed into law
events.description: On 4 February 1887, President Grover Cleveland signed the Interstate Commerce Act, creating the Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC) — the first independent federal regulatory agency in the United States. The law required railroad rates to be 'reasonable and just,' prohibited rate discrimination between long and short hauls, banned pooling arrangements, and mandated public tariff schedules. It was the culmination of over a decade of agrarian and merchant agitation against railroad monopoly pricing, particularly from Granger and Populist movements. Though initially weak in enforcement — railroads quickly learned to exploit legal ambiguities — the ICC established the principle of federal economic regulation and became the template for all subsequent regulatory agencies.

sources.name: Interstate Commerce Act, 49th Congress, Sess. II, Ch. 104 (1887)
Congressional Record debates on the Cullom Bill, 1886–1887
Ari Hoogenboom and Olive Hoogenboom, A History of the ICC: From Panacea to Palliative (1976)

events.certainty_note: Fully documented in federal legislative records, contemporary press, and extensive historiographic literature.

events.title: Dawes General Allotment Act
events.description: Signed on 8 February 1887, the Dawes Act authorised the President to survey and divide Native American tribal lands into individual allotments — 160 acres to each family head, 80 to single adults — with 'surplus' land opened to white settlement. Framed by reformers like Senator Henry Dawes as a tool of assimilation that would turn Native Americans into yeoman farmers, the act was catastrophic for Indigenous communities. Over the next four decades, tribal landholdings shrank from approximately 138 million acres to 48 million acres. The act undermined collective governance, disrupted cultural practices tied to communal land use, and transferred enormous wealth to white settlers and speculators. It remained the cornerstone of federal Indian policy until reversed by the Indian Reorganization Act of 1934.

sources.name: Dawes General Allotment Act, 49th Congress, Sess. II, Ch. 119 (1887)
D. S. Otis, The Dawes Act and the Allotment of Indian Lands (1934; reprinted 1973)
Frederick E. Hoxie, A Final Promise: The Campaign to Assimilate the Indians, 1880–1920 (1984)

events.certainty_note: Fully documented in federal legislative records and exhaustive historiographic literature. Land loss figures are well established from General Land Office records.

events.title: Battle of Dogali — Ethiopian victory over Italian forces
events.description: On 26 January 1887, a force of approximately 500 Italian soldiers under Lieutenant Colonel Tommaso De Cristoforis was ambushed and annihilated by an Ethiopian army led by Ras Alula Engida near the village of Dogali in present-day Eritrea. Some 430 Italians were killed. The battle was a response to Italian expansion inland from the Red Sea port of Massawa, which the Ethiopians viewed as encroachment on their sovereignty. In Italy, the defeat provoked a surge of nationalist outrage and demands for revenge, setting the stage for escalating Italian colonial ambitions that would culminate in the disastrous Battle of Adwa in 1896. For Ethiopia, it demonstrated the military effectiveness of Ras Alula and reinforced Emperor Yohannes IV's determination to resist European encroachment.
key_figures: Tommaso De Cristoforis (Italian lieutenant colonel, killed)

sources.name: Italian War Ministry reports and parliamentary debates on the Dogali engagement, 1887
Haggai Erlich, Ras Alula and the Scramble for Africa (1996)
Harold G. Marcus, A History of Ethiopia (1994)

events.certainty_note: Well documented from both Italian military records and Ethiopian sources. Exact Ethiopian casualty figures are less certain, but the Italian losses and the outcome are undisputed.

events.title: Reinsurance Treaty between Germany and Russia
events.description: On 18 June 1887, Germany and Russia signed the secret Reinsurance Treaty, replacing the expired Three Emperors' League. The treaty committed each party to benevolent neutrality if the other went to war with a third great power, except in the case of a German attack on France or a Russian attack on Austria-Hungary. A secret protocol acknowledged Russia's interests in Bulgaria and the Straits. Bismarck considered it essential to prevent a Franco-Russian alliance, maintaining Germany's pivotal position in European diplomacy even as tensions between Austria-Hungary and Russia over the Balkans made the earlier tripartite arrangement untenable. The treaty's lapse after Bismarck's dismissal in 1890 is widely regarded as one of the critical diplomatic errors leading to the polarisation of Europe before 1914.

sources.name: Text of the Reinsurance Treaty, 18 June 1887 (published by Bismarck in 1896)
George F. Kennan, The Decline of Bismarck's European Order: Franco-Russian Relations, 1875–1890 (1979)
William L. Langer, European Alliances and Alignments, 1871–1890 (1931; rev. 1950)

events.certainty_note: Text of the treaty is extant. Diplomatic context extensively documented in German and Russian archives.

events.title: Mediterranean Agreements between Britain, Italy, and Austria-Hungary
events.description: In February and December 1887, Britain exchanged notes with Italy and Austria-Hungary committing to maintain the status quo in the Mediterranean, Adriatic, Aegean, and Black Seas. The First Mediterranean Agreement (February) linked Britain and Italy, later joined by Austria-Hungary and Spain; the Second (December) was more specifically aimed at blocking Russian expansion toward the Straits and French moves in North Africa. Though not formal alliances — Britain avoided binding treaty commitments — these agreements marked a significant departure from British isolation and aligned British interests with the Triple Alliance powers against France and Russia. Bismarck encouraged the arrangements as part of his strategy to contain Russia without Germany bearing the full burden.
key_figures: Luigi di Robilant / Francesco Crispi (Italian foreign policy leaders)

sources.name: Diplomatic correspondence published in Die Grosse Politik der Europäischen Kabinette, 1871–1914
C. J. Lowe, Salisbury and the Mediterranean, 1886–1896 (1965)

events.certainty_note: Documented in published diplomatic correspondence of the relevant foreign offices. The informal nature of the notes (not ratified treaties) has led to historiographic debate about their binding force.

events.title: Yellow River catastrophic flood
events.description: In September 1887, the Yellow River (Huang He) breached its southern dyke near Zhengzhou in Henan province, inundating an area of approximately 50,000 square kilometres across Henan, Shandong, and parts of Anhui and Jiangsu. Contemporary and later estimates of the death toll range widely, from 900,000 to over 2 million, making it one of the deadliest natural disasters in recorded history. The flood destroyed crops, villages, and the economic base of one of China's most densely populated agricultural regions. The Qing government's relief efforts were hampered by fiscal weakness, corruption, and the sheer scale of the disaster, contributing to growing popular disillusionment with the dynasty. International relief efforts, coordinated partly through treaty-port communities, marked an early instance of organised international humanitarian aid to China.
key_figures: Li Hongzhang (Viceroy of Zhili, involved in relief coordination)

sources.name: Qing dynasty official memorials and relief records (Qing shilu)
Contemporary reports in the North-China Herald and other treaty-port press, September–November 1887
Kathryn Edgerton-Tarpley, Tears from Iron: Cultural Responses to Famine in Nineteenth-Century China (2008)
Pietz, David A., The Yellow River: The Problem of Water in Modern China (2015)

events.certainty_note: The flood is extensively documented in Chinese official records and contemporary Western press. Exact death toll is uncertain and varies dramatically between sources, but the catastrophic scale is undisputed.

events.title: Queen Victoria's Golden Jubilee
events.description: On 20–21 June 1887, the British Empire celebrated the fiftieth anniversary of Queen Victoria's accession to the throne with a series of public events centred on a procession through London and a service at Westminster Abbey. The jubilee was attended by heads of state and representatives from across Europe and the Empire, serving as both a personal celebration and a demonstration of British imperial power at its zenith. It coincided with the First Colonial Conference (April 1887), at which colonial premiers discussed imperial defence, trade, and communications. The jubilee reinforced Victoria's symbolic role as the embodiment of empire and prompted a wave of public commemorative projects — hospitals, parks, monuments — across the colonial world.
key_figures: Crown Prince Frederick of Germany (Victoria's son-in-law)

sources.name: Queen Victoria's journal entries, June 1887 (published in part in Letters of Queen Victoria)
Contemporary coverage in The Times, Illustrated London News, and colonial press
Jan Morris, Pax Britannica: The Climax of an Empire (1968)

events.certainty_note: Exhaustively documented in contemporary sources across the British Empire.

events.title: First Colonial Conference in London
events.description: In April 1887, coinciding with the Golden Jubilee preparations, the first Colonial Conference brought together the premiers of the self-governing colonies — Canada, the Australian colonies, New Zealand, the Cape Colony, and Natal — with the British government. Discussion focused on imperial defence (particularly naval contributions), communications (undersea telegraph cables), and trade preferences. While no binding decisions emerged, the conference established the precedent for regular imperial consultation that evolved into the Imperial Conferences and ultimately the modern Commonwealth. It reflected both the growing self-assertion of the settler colonies and the imperial government's desire to share the costs of global defence.
key_figures: Sir John A. Macdonald (Canadian Prime Minister, absent but represented)

sources.name: Proceedings of the Colonial Conference, 1887 (Parliamentary Papers, C. 5091)
John E. Kendle, The Colonial and Imperial Conferences, 1887–1911 (1967)

events.certainty_note: Official proceedings are published in British Parliamentary Papers.

events.title: Bloody Sunday in Trafalgar Square
events.description: On 13 November 1887, a mass demonstration in Trafalgar Square protesting British coercion policies in Ireland and demanding the release of the Irish MP William O'Brien was violently dispersed by police and troops. An estimated 10,000–20,000 demonstrators — socialists, radicals, Irish nationalists, and unemployed workers — converged on the square, where they were met by 2,000 police, 300 mounted Life Guards, and 300 Grenadier Guards with fixed bayonets. Over 150 people were injured and two later died of their injuries. The event radicalised many participants, including the young Social Democratic Federation activist John Burns and the journalist Annie Besant, and demonstrated the willingness of Salisbury's Conservative government to suppress political dissent by force.

sources.name: Contemporary reports in The Times, Pall Mall Gazette, and Reynolds's Newspaper, November 1887
Donald C. Richter, Riotous Victorians (1981)

events.certainty_note: Extensively covered in contemporary press and parliamentary records. Casualty figures vary slightly between sources.

events.title: Formation of French Indochina
events.description: On 17 October 1887, a decree by French President Sadi Carnot created the Indochinese Union (Union indochinoise), consolidating the colony of Cochinchina, the protectorates of Annam, Tonkin, and Cambodia under a single Governor-General based in Saigon (later Hanoi). Laos would be added in 1893. The administrative unification marked the maturation of France's Southeast Asian empire, which had been assembled piecemeal through military campaigns since the 1850s. It imposed a centralised colonial bureaucracy over diverse peoples and polities, established the framework for French economic exploitation — particularly through rice exports, rubber plantations, and opium and alcohol monopolies — and created the political geography that would shape the region's twentieth-century conflicts.
key_figures: Paul Bert (predecessor Resident-General of Annam-Tonkin, died 1886)

sources.name: Décret portant création de l'Union indochinoise, 17 October 1887 (Journal officiel de la République française)
Pierre Brocheux and Daniel Hémery, Indochina: An Ambiguous Colonization, 1858–1954 (English trans. 2009)

events.certainty_note: Administrative decree is extant in French official records.

events.title: Michelson–Morley experiment
events.description: In 1887, Albert A. Michelson and Edward W. Morley conducted their famous interferometer experiment at the Case School of Applied Science in Cleveland, attempting to detect the motion of the Earth through the hypothetical luminiferous aether — the medium then believed to carry light waves. The experiment found no significant difference in the speed of light regardless of direction, producing a null result that contradicted the predictions of aether theory. Although its immediate impact on physics was gradual rather than revolutionary — many physicists initially sought to save the aether through ad hoc hypotheses like the Lorentz–FitzGerald contraction — the experiment became a cornerstone of Einstein's 1905 special theory of relativity and is regarded as one of the most important null results in the history of science.
key_figures: Edward W. Morley (chemist, Western Reserve University)

sources.name: A. A. Michelson and E. W. Morley, 'On the Relative Motion of the Earth and the Luminiferous Ether,' American Journal of Science, 34 (1887), pp. 333–345
Loyd S. Swenson Jr., The Ethereal Aether: A History of the Michelson-Morley-Miller Aether-Drift Experiments, 1880–1930 (1972)

events.certainty_note: Original paper is extant and widely reproduced. The experiment has been replicated many times with consistent null results.

events.title: Heinrich Hertz demonstrates electromagnetic waves
events.description: In 1887, Heinrich Hertz at the Karlsruhe Polytechnic began a series of experiments that provided the first conclusive experimental confirmation of James Clerk Maxwell's electromagnetic theory. Using a spark-gap transmitter and a loop antenna receiver, Hertz generated and detected radio-frequency electromagnetic waves, demonstrating that they exhibited reflection, refraction, polarisation, and interference — behaving exactly as Maxwell's equations predicted. He also discovered the photoelectric effect during these experiments, though he did not pursue its theoretical implications. Hertz's work, published in full between 1887 and 1890, established the physical reality of electromagnetic radiation beyond visible light and laid the experimental foundation for radio communication, developed commercially by Marconi and others within the next decade.
key_figures: Hermann von Helmholtz (mentor, suggested the research programme)

sources.name: Heinrich Hertz, 'Ueber sehr schnelle electrische Schwingungen,' Annalen der Physik, 267 (1887), pp. 421–448
Heinrich Hertz, Electric Waves (English trans. by D. E. Jones, 1893) — collected experimental papers
Jed Z. Buchwald, The Creation of Scientific Effects: Heinrich Hertz and Electric Waves (1994)

events.certainty_note: Original publications are extant. The precise chronology of Hertz's experiments spans 1886–1888; the decisive wave-demonstration results are from late 1887 into 1888.

events.title: Construction of the Eiffel Tower begins
events.description: On 28 January 1887, construction began on the Eiffel Tower on the Champ de Mars in Paris, intended as the centrepiece of the 1889 Exposition Universelle celebrating the centenary of the French Revolution. Designed by the engineer Gustave Eiffel's firm (with key structural calculations by Maurice Koechlin and Émile Nouguier), the wrought-iron lattice tower would reach 300 metres — making it the tallest man-made structure in the world, surpassing the Washington Monument. The project provoked fierce opposition from prominent Parisian artists and intellectuals who signed the 'Protestation des artistes' calling it an industrial eyesore. Nevertheless, it was completed in March 1889, employing some 300 workers and using 7,300 tonnes of iron assembled with 2.5 million rivets.

sources.name: Gustave Eiffel, La Tour de Trois Cents Mètres (1900) — Eiffel's own account of the construction
'Protestation des artistes,' published in Le Temps, 14 February 1887
Joseph Harriss, The Tallest Tower: Eiffel and the Belle Epoque (1975)

events.certainty_note: Exhaustively documented in construction records, press coverage, and Eiffel's own publications.

events.title: Émile Berliner patents the gramophone
events.description: On 26 September 1887, the German-born American inventor Émile Berliner filed a patent for the gramophone, a device that played sound recorded on flat discs rather than the cylinders used by Thomas Edison's phonograph. Berliner's lateral-cut disc format proved far more practical for mass production — discs could be stamped from a master, while cylinders had to be individually recorded or moulded. Though commercial exploitation took several more years, the gramophone disc format ultimately won the format war against Edison's cylinders by the early 1900s and became the dominant medium for recorded music for nearly a century, fundamentally transforming the music industry, popular culture, and the global dissemination of sound.

sources.name: U.S. Patent No. 372,786, filed 26 September 1887, granted 8 November 1887
Berliner's presentation to the Franklin Institute, 16 May 1888 (published in the Institute's Journal)
Roland Gelatt, The Fabulous Phonograph, 1877–1977 (1977)

events.certainty_note: Patent records and Berliner's own presentations provide precise documentation.

events.title: Publication of A Study in Scarlet — Sherlock Holmes debut
events.description: In November 1887, Arthur Conan Doyle's novella A Study in Scarlet appeared in Beeton's Christmas Annual, introducing the detective Sherlock Holmes and his companion Dr John Watson to the reading public. The initial publication attracted modest attention and earned Doyle just £25 for the copyright, but the characters would go on to become among the most recognisable in world literature. Holmes's method of scientific deduction — drawing on forensic evidence, logical reasoning, and close observation — reflected and popularised the positivist and empirical spirit of the age. The detective fiction genre, while not invented by Doyle, was decisively shaped by the Holmes stories into a mass-market literary form.

sources.name: A Study in Scarlet, published in Beeton's Christmas Annual, November 1887
Andrew Lycett, Conan Doyle: The Man Who Created Sherlock Holmes (2007)

events.certainty_note: Publication date and circumstances are well documented. Surviving copies of Beeton's Christmas Annual 1887 are among the rarest items in book collecting.

events.title: Hatch Act establishes agricultural experiment stations
events.description: Signed on 2 March 1887, the Hatch Act provided federal funding for agricultural experiment stations in each state, to be affiliated with the land-grant colleges established under the Morrill Act of 1862. The stations would conduct original research into soil science, crop varieties, animal husbandry, and pest control, then disseminate findings to farmers. This created one of the world's first systematic government-funded scientific research networks and laid the institutional foundation for the agricultural productivity gains that would transform American farming in the twentieth century. The act represented an early and successful model of government-sponsored applied science.
key_figures: Seaman A. Knapp (agricultural reformer and advocate)

sources.name: Hatch Act of 1887 (49 Stat. 440)
Alan I Marcus, Agricultural Science and the Quest for Legitimacy (1985)

events.certainty_note: Federal legislative record; institutional history well documented.

events.title: Boulanger Crisis escalates in France
events.description: Throughout 1887, the political crisis surrounding General Georges Boulanger intensified. Having been appointed War Minister in January 1886, Boulanger cultivated enormous popular appeal through nationalist rhetoric, military reforms, and calls for revenge against Germany (revanchisme). His popularity alarmed both the republican establishment and Germany — the Schnaebele incident of April 1887, in which a French official was arrested on the German border, nearly provoked a war crisis partly because of Boulanger's bellicosity. In May 1887 he was dropped from the cabinet but continued to build a populist movement that united discontented elements from left and right. The crisis tested the stability of the Third Republic and foreshadowed later right-wing populist movements in France.
key_figures: Georges Clemenceau (initially a Boulanger supporter, then opponent)

sources.name: Contemporary French press coverage (Le Figaro, La Lanterne, L'Intransigeant), 1887
William D. Irvine, The Boulanger Affair Reconsidered (1989)
Frédéric Seager, The Boulanger Affair: Political Crossroad of France, 1886–1889 (1969)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in French parliamentary records, press, and diplomatic archives.

events.title: First Sino-Portuguese Treaty — Portugal formalises control of Macau
events.description: On 1 December 1887, the Qing government and Portugal signed the Sino-Portuguese Treaty of Peking, by which China formally recognised Portugal's 'perpetual occupation and government' of Macau and its dependencies. Portugal had administered Macau since the mid-sixteenth century, but Chinese sovereignty had never been formally renounced. In exchange, Portugal agreed never to alienate Macau to a third power without Chinese consent. The treaty regularised a centuries-old anomaly and established the legal framework that would govern Macau's status until its handover to China in 1999.

sources.name: Treaty text in British and Foreign State Papers, Vol. 79
Geoffrey C. Gunn, Encountering Macau: A Portuguese City-State on the Periphery of China, 1557–1999 (1996)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text is extant in diplomatic archives of both nations and published in international law compilations.

events.title: Zulu territories annexed by Britain
events.description: In 1887, the British government formally annexed Zululand as a crown colony, completing the subjugation of the Zulu kingdom that had been effectively dismembered since the Anglo-Zulu War of 1879. After the war, the British had divided Zululand among thirteen appointed chiefs, a system that produced chronic instability and civil conflict. Annexation placed the territory under direct British administration and opened it to white settlement and land speculation, further dispossessing the Zulu people. In 1897, Zululand would be incorporated into the colony of Natal.
key_figures: Dinuzulu kaCetshwayo (Zulu prince, resisted British authority)

sources.name: British Colonial Office correspondence and proclamation of annexation, 1887
Jeff Guy, The Destruction of the Zulu Kingdom (1979)

events.certainty_note: Colonial Office records provide full documentation. Exact dates of administrative implementation varied across the territory.

events.title: Japan's treaty revision crisis and the Normanton incident aftermath
events.description: In 1887, Foreign Minister Inoue Kaoru's prolonged negotiations to revise the unequal treaties imposed on Japan in the 1850s–1860s collapsed under fierce domestic opposition. Inoue's draft agreements, which would have opened the Japanese interior to foreign residence and employed foreign judges in mixed courts, were seen as surrendering too much sovereignty. The backlash was intensified by lingering outrage over the 1886 Normanton incident, in which a British captain abandoned Japanese passengers to drown while saving European crew, then was acquitted by a consular court. In December 1887, the government issued the Peace Preservation Law to suppress political agitation, banishing hundreds of opposition figures from Tokyo. Treaty revision would not be achieved until Mutsu Munemitsu's negotiations in 1894.

sources.name: Japanese Foreign Ministry records (Nihon gaikō bunsho) for 1887
Louis G. Perez, Japan Comes of Age: Mutsu Munemitsu and the Revision of the Unequal Treaties (1999)
Marius B. Jansen, The Making of Modern Japan (2000)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in Japanese diplomatic archives and contemporary press. The Peace Preservation Law text is extant.

events.title: Menelik II of Shewa consolidates power in Ethiopia
events.description: While Emperor Yohannes IV was occupied with fighting the Mahdists on the Sudan border and containing Italian encroachment from the Red Sea coast, King Menelik II of Shewa continued expanding his power base in central and southern Ethiopia. In 1887, Menelik conducted major military campaigns to consolidate control over Harar (conquered January 1887 after the Battle of Chelenqo) and surrounding Oromo and Somali territories. The conquest of Harar, one of the most important trading cities in the Horn of Africa, dramatically expanded Menelik's revenue base and strategic position. These campaigns laid the territorial foundation for the expanded Ethiopian state that Menelik would rule as Emperor after Yohannes's death in 1889.
key_figures: Menelik II (King of Shewa, future Emperor)

sources.name: Contemporary accounts by European travellers and diplomats in Shewa, including Alfred Ilg's correspondence
Harold G. Marcus, The Life and Times of Menelik II: Ethiopia, 1844–1913 (1975)
Richard Pankhurst, Economic History of Ethiopia, 1800–1935 (1968)

events.certainty_note: Well documented from both European diplomatic sources and Ethiopian chronicles. The Battle of Chelenqo and conquest of Harar are firmly dated to January 1887.

events.title: Brazil's abolitionist movement intensifies — mass slave flight
events.description: By 1887, the abolitionist movement in Brazil had reached a tipping point as mass slave flight from plantations in São Paulo and other provinces effectively began destroying the institution from below. Abolitionist networks, including the Underground Railroad-like organisation run by Antônio Bento and the Caifazes in São Paulo, actively encouraged and facilitated escape. The Brazilian army increasingly refused to pursue fugitive slaves, with several officers publicly declaring their unwillingness to serve as slave catchers. Plantation owners in São Paulo and other regions began manumitting slaves en masse, attempting to convert them into wage labourers before losing them entirely. This popular revolt against slavery from multiple sectors of society set the stage for the Lei Áurea (Golden Law) of 13 May 1888, which would formally abolish slavery.
key_figures: Princess Isabel (regent, future signer of the Golden Law)

sources.name: Contemporary Brazilian press coverage in Gazeta da Tarde and Cidade do Rio, 1887
Robert Conrad, The Destruction of Brazilian Slavery, 1850–1888 (1972)
Seymour Drescher, Abolition: A History of Slavery and Antislavery (2009)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in Brazilian press, provincial government records, and police reports. The scale of flight in 1887 is well attested by multiple sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The Michelson–Morley experiment (1887-011) is often presented in popular science narratives as a direct inspiration for Einstein's special relativity, but Einstein himself gave contradictory accounts of its influence on his thinking, and some historians of science (notably Gerald Holton) have argued that Einstein may have developed his theory largely independently of the experimental result. The experiment's significance was arguably constructed retrospectively. Additionally, the Yellow River flood death toll (1887-006) is frequently cited as '900,000' or even '2 million' but these figures are extremely difficult to verify; modern hydraulic historians note that the original Qing sources do not provide systematic casualty counts, and the widely circulated figures may derive from rough contemporary estimates amplified through repetition. The Dawes Act (1887-002) is sometimes characterised as purely a land-grab scheme, but recent scholarship (e.g., Hoxie 1984) has emphasised that many of its most ardent supporters were genuine, if deeply paternalistic, reformers who believed allotment would protect Native Americans from removal and extinction — the act's catastrophic consequences were not entirely foreseen by its proponents, though critics like the Choctaw leader Pleasant Porter warned against it at the time.
historiographic_note: 1887 falls in a period of exceptionally rich documentation for European, American, and East Asian history, with extensive official archives, press coverage, diplomatic correspondence, and early photographic records. However, the historical record is heavily skewed toward state and elite perspectives. Coverage of Sub-Saharan Africa depends overwhelmingly on European colonial sources, with African perspectives recoverable mainly through oral tradition and later compilation. Southeast Asian internal perspectives are filtered through French colonial records. The Chinese record, while extensive, prioritises court and official perspectives; peasant experiences of the Yellow River flood are largely unrecorded. Latin American coverage is heavily concentrated on capital-city politics and export economies. Indigenous perspectives within settler-colonial states are systematically underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: The ICC established the federal regulatory precedent that the Sherman Antitrust Act extended to monopoly practices more broadly
The Dawes Act's devastating effects on tribal land and governance were reversed by the Wheeler-Howard Act of 1934
Italian desire to avenge Dogali contributed to escalating colonial ambitions that culminated in the decisive Ethiopian victory at Adwa
The Reinsurance Treaty lapsed in 1890 after Bismarck's dismissal, opening the path to the Franco-Russian Alliance
The 1855 northward course change of the Yellow River created unstable levee systems that failed catastrophically in 1887
The null result undermined aether theory and became a key experimental foundation for Einstein's special relativity
Hertz's demonstration of electromagnetic waves made wireless telegraphy conceivable, enabling Marconi's practical radio experiments
The Eiffel Tower was built specifically as the centrepiece of the 1889 Paris Exposition
The Boulanger movement reached its peak in 1888–1889 before collapsing when Boulanger fled France
The failure of Inoue's treaty revision efforts in 1887 left the task to Mutsu Munemitsu, who succeeded in 1894
Menelik's territorial consolidation in 1887 built the resource base and military strength that enabled his victory at Adwa
Mass slave flight and institutional collapse of enforcement in 1887 made formal abolition in May 1888 inevitable
